constants.e: f s g n a b l m o w bi st pi tm el sr mr x1 x2 r

variables.et: X Y Z P Q R F G H

exercises.title: A. Intransitive verbs
exercises.instructions: A subject of type e combines with an intransitive verb of type ⟨e,t⟩ by Function Application, yielding a proposition of type t.

exercises.title: B. Transitive and ditransitive verbs
exercises.instructions: A transitive verb ⟨e,⟨e,t⟩⟩ takes its object first, then its subject. A ditransitive verb takes three arguments in turn; here "the ring" is treated as a single individual of type e (definite descriptions come in §8).

exercises.title: C. The copula
exercises.instructions: The copula "is" and the article "a" are the identity function λX.X. They pass the predicate up unchanged, and the predicate applies to the subject.

exercises.title: D. Prepositions
exercises.instructions: A preposition is a relation of type ⟨e,⟨e,t⟩⟩, like a transitive verb. It combines with its complement to form a predicate ⟨e,t⟩, which the copula passes up to the subject.

exercises.title: E. Negation
exercises.instructions: Negation is type ⟨⟨e,t⟩,⟨e,t⟩⟩: it takes a predicate and returns its complement. "doesn't" negates a verb phrase; "not" after the copula negates the predicate nominal.

reading.markdown:
# Chapter 6 · Function Application

Frege's insight is that a verb phrase is *unsaturated*: `loves Frodo` is a meaning with a hole in it, waiting for a subject. The guiding hypothesis of this chapter is that meanings combine in essentially **one** way — a function meets its argument and fills the hole.

We translate each English word into the typed lambda calculus, give every node in the tree a semantic **type**, and let two composition rules do all the work.

## 6.1 Function Application

There are two basic types: $e$ for individuals and $t$ for truth values. A one-place predicate is a function from individuals to truth values, written $<e,t>$.

**Function Application (FA).** If a branching node has two daughters — one of type $<s,t>$ and the other of type $s$ — the mother denotes the result of applying the first to the second, and has type $t$.[^order]

**Non-branching Nodes (NN).** A node with a single daughter simply inherits that daughter's denotation.

An intransitive verb is type $<e,t>$ and a subject name is type $e$, so the two meet directly by FA:

```deriv
[[runs]]       = lambda x.run(x)  : <e,t>
[[Frodo]]      = f                : e
[[Frodo runs]] = run(f)           : t
```

```tree
[S{run(f)}
  [DP{f} Frodo]
  [VP{lambda x.run(x)} runs]]
```

So *Frodo runs* is true exactly when $f$ belongs to the set of runners. Every sentence in (@intrans) has this same shape.

```ex {#intrans}
Frodo runs.
Strider hikes.
* Runs Frodo.
```

[^order]: $s$ and $t$ here are *any* types, not just the truth-value type — they range over whatever the two daughters happen to be. FA only cares that one daughter's input type matches the other daughter's type.

## 6.2 Transitive and ditransitive verbs

A transitive verb wants two arguments, so it is type $<e,<e,t>>$ — a function that takes the **object** first and returns a one-place predicate:

```deriv
[[loves]]              = lambda y.lambda x.love(x,y) : <e,<e,t>>
[[loves Frodo]]        = lambda x.love(x,f)          : <e,t>
[[Bilbo loves Frodo]]  = love(bi,f)                  : t
```

```tree
[S{love(bi,f)}
  [DP{bi} Bilbo]
  [VP{lambda x.love(x,f)}
    [V{lambda y.lambda x.love(x,y)} loves]
    [DP{f} Frodo]]]
```

The object saturates the inner $lambda y$; the subject saturates the outer $lambda x$ at the top node. A ditransitive verb such as *gives* is type $<e,<e,<e,t>>>$ and peels off three arguments in turn, innermost first.

## 6.2.2 The copula and predication

Words like *is* and the article *a* carry no content of their own: each is the identity function $lambda X.X$, of type $<<e,t>,<e,t>>$. They pass a predicate up the tree untouched, so the predicate ends up applying to the subject:

```deriv
[[is]] = [[a]]         = lambda X.X         : <<e,t>,<e,t>>
[[hobbit]]             = lambda x.hobbit(x) : <e,t>
[[Frodo is a hobbit]]  = hobbit(f)          : t
```

A predicate adjective behaves the same way: *Sam is brave* reduces all the way to $brave(s)$.

## 6.2.3 Prepositions

A preposition is a relation between two individuals, type $<e,<e,t>>$ — structurally just like a transitive verb. It combines with its complement to form a predicate, which the copula then relays to the subject:

```deriv
[[with]]               = lambda y.lambda x.with(x,y) : <e,<e,t>>
[[with Frodo]]         = lambda x.with(x,f)          : <e,t>
[[Sam is with Frodo]]  = with(s,f)                   : t
```

## 6.3 Negation

Predicate negation is type $<<e,t>,<e,t>>$: it takes a predicate and returns its **complement** — the set of everything the predicate is false of.

```deriv
[[doesnt]] = [[not]]      = lambda P.lambda x.~P(x) : <<e,t>,<e,t>>
[[doesnt fight]]          = lambda x.~fight(x)      : <e,t>
[[Frodo doesnt fight]]    = ~fight(f)               : t
```

*doesn't* negates a verb phrase; *not* after the copula negates a predicate nominal or PP, as in (@neg). Either way the result is the complement of the predicate it applies to.

```ex {#neg}
Frodo doesn't fight.
Sauron is not a hobbit.
Sauron is not in Rivendell.
```
